items.myth: F1 = 0.90 means the model is ready for any deployment context.
items.reality: F1 embeds one threshold and equal weight on precision and recall; a fraud desk needing 99% precision may reject a 0.90 F1 model whose recall-driven threshold generates too many alerts.
items.example: F1 0.88 at threshold 0.5 might be 0.72 F1 at the precision floor the operations team requires.

items.myth: Precision and recall are independent of the threshold.
items.reality: Both are computed at a fixed cutoff on scores or hard class labels; changing the threshold moves you along a precision–recall trade-off curve.
items.example: Lowering threshold from 0.8 to 0.3 raises recall from 0.55 to 0.82 while precision falls from 0.91 to 0.67.

items.myth: High recall always implies a good model.
items.reality: Predicting every example as positive yields recall = 1 but precision equal to the positive base rate—useless when negatives dominate.
items.example: 1% positive rate: predict-all-positive gives recall 1.0 and precision 0.01.

items.myth: Accuracy and F1 always rank models the same way.
items.reality: Accuracy weights all errors equally; F1 ignores true negatives and focuses on positive-class errors—rankings diverge on skewed data.
items.example: Model A: accuracy 0.96, F1 0.40 on rare disease; Model B: accuracy 0.94, F1 0.62—F1 picks B, accuracy picks A.
